ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for gyudon (Japanese beef rice bowl). FOODON_00002809 (beef dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for gyudon as a composite dish. A0CKP (Beef, cooked) is used as the closest parent anchor for the primary protein component. The dish also contains rice, egg, daikon, and ponzu sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for gyudon or Japanese beef rice bowl as a composite food item.

allergens: fish (ponzu contains citrus juice and typically dashi from bonito)

serving.description: 1 bowl (standard gyudon serving with egg and daikon)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with simmered beef and onion, grated and diced daikon radish, a half-boiled egg, and ponzu sauce drizzled over the top, served as a combo with miso soup or a drink.

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef bowl) — composite nutrient estimation based on USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked beef (SR Legacy #13397), cooked white rice (SR Legacy #20045), soft-boiled egg (SR Legacy #01129), daikon radish raw (SR Legacy #11429), and ponzu sauce (USDA Branded Foods reference); cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Japanese-style beef rice bowl analogues.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish (gyudon with half-boiled egg, diced and grated daikon, and ponzu sauce). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly: approximately 180 g steamed white rice, 120 g simmered beef slices with onion and cooking sauce, 50 g half-boiled egg, 60 g daikon (mixed grated and diced), and 40 g ponzu sauce, scaled to per-100 g. Values are consistent with published gyudon nutritional profiles from Japanese food composition literature.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked) GI ~54–72; composite dish GI estimated at 54 based on dominant carbohydrate source (white rice) moderated by protein, fat, and vinegar/citrus content of ponzu sauce.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021: A Systematic Review. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for gyudon with ponzu and daikon. GI estimated at 54 (low) using Japanese short-grain white rice as the primary glycemic driver (GI ~54 per University of Sydney GI Database entry for Japanese rice). The acidic ponzu sauce (citrus and vinegar components) and the protein and fat from beef and egg are known to attenuate postprandial glucose response, supporting the lower end of the low GI range. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 71.1 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 38.4, rounded to 38.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, dashi, and ponzu sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,395 mg per standard 450 g serving), which represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce or sauce-on-the-side options may help lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 54) but high glycemic load per serving (approximately 38) due to large portion of white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that this dish has a low glycemic index (estimated GI 54) but a high glycemic load per serving (approximately 38) due to the large portion of white rice; portion control, substituting brown rice where available, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and egg combined with high sodium from seasoning sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation, as the combination of saturated fat (approximately 1.6 g per 100 g from beef and egg) and high sodium from soy-based and ponzu sauces may contribute to elevated LDL cholesterol and blood pressure; choosing a smaller portion and requesting lighter sauce application is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half-Boiled Egg, Diced and Grated Daikon Gyudon with Ponzu Sauce is a Japanese beef rice bowl (gyudon) variant that layers thinly sliced, simmered beef and onion over a bowl of steamed short-grain white rice, topped with a combination of finely grated daikon radish (daikon oroshi) and diced daikon pieces, a soft half-boiled egg (hanjuku tamago) with a runny yolk, and a drizzle of ponzu sauce — a light, citrus-forward Japanese condiment made from soy sauce, yuzu or sudachi juice, mirin, and dashi. The dish is served as a combo meal at Japanese fast-casual chains such as Sukiya, accompanied by miso soup or a drink. The flavour profile is savoury and umami-rich from the beef and dashi-based cooking broth, with a refreshing, tangy brightness from the ponzu and the cooling, slightly peppery bite of the daikon. The half-boiled egg adds a creamy, rich textural contrast. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (approximately 35 g per serving) from beef and egg, significant carbohydrate from white rice (approximately 71 g per serving), and notable sodium from the soy-based sauces. The daikon contributes dietary fibre, vitamin C, and digestive enzymes (diastase). Clinically, the dish has a low glycemic index but high glycemic load, making it of moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes, and of high concern for hypertension due to its sodium content. It is suitable for non-vegetarian, non-vegan, and non-halal diets. The ponzu sauce typically contains fish-derived dashi, making it unsuitable for strict vegetarians and vegans.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components (cooked beef, white rice, soft-boiled egg, daikon radish, ponzu sauce). GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved GI source (Japanese short-grain white rice, University of Sydney GI Database) with documented moderation factors. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated (not from a single direct source entry) using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for each ingredient, scaled to a 450 g serving and back-calculated to per-100 g. 2. GI is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain white rice (GI 54, University of Sydney GI Database) as the primary glycemic driver, with documented attenuation from ponzu acid content and macronutrient co-ingestion. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component USDA SR Legacy data and should be treated as approximate. 4. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains beef from a non-certified source and ponzu sauce with fish-derived dashi. 5. Ponzu sauce contains citrus juice (yuzu or sudachi), soy sauce, mirin, and bonito/kombu dashi — allergen note includes fish and soy. 6. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON (Japanese Curry, Grilled Chicken Skewers, Fried Chicken) appear to be restaurant-level tags for Sukiya's broader menu and are not applicable to this specific dish; they have been excluded from this record. 7. No -1 sentinel values require special justification beyond the standard note that these micronutrient fields are estimated from component data rather than directly reported by a single source entry for this composite dish.
